claim: Tokenization breaks input text into discrete semantic units (tokens) rather than characters, allowing LLMs to understand suffixes like -ers and -ing that carry meaning
actionable: When designing prompts or analyzing LLM behavior, think in terms of tokens (semantic units) rather than words or characters

claim: Vectorization maps words to n-dimensional coordinates where semantically similar words cluster together, enabling meaning-based operations on text
actionable: Leverage vector similarity for semantic search and retrieval; use vector databases to find contextually relevant documents without keyword matching

claim: Attention mechanism resolves ambiguous tokens (like 'apple' meaning fruit vs company) by adding contextual vectors from nearby words to disambiguate meaning
actionable: When prompting, provide surrounding context for ambiguous terms to help attention mechanisms correctly disambiguate meaning

claim: Self-supervised learning enables cheap LLM training by creating prediction tasks from raw text structure without human labels
actionable: Use self-supervised techniques when training custom models on domain data; create prediction tasks from text structure rather than hiring annotators

claim: Transformers stack multiple attention layers (12-100+ deep) to build up complex relationships like sarcasm and inference from tokenized input
actionable: Understand that deeper transformer stacks capture more abstract relationships; use larger models when reasoning or sarcasm detection is needed

claim: Fine-tuning adjusts a pre-trained base model using domain-specific Q&A pairs to make it speak in domain jargon and follow specific response formats
actionable: Fine-tune base models on domain-specific Q&A data (medical, financial) to improve terminology accuracy and response format adherence

claim: Few-shot prompting adds example Q&A pairs at inference time to improve response quality without retraining the model
actionable: Include 2-5 well-chosen examples in prompts to guide model behavior at runtime without fine-tuning costs

claim: RAG combines LLMs with vector database retrieval of relevant documents to ground responses in company-specific policies and knowledge
actionable: Implement RAG by storing company docs in vector DB, retrieving relevant context at inference time, and appending to LLM prompts

claim: Vector databases enable semantic search by storing document embeddings and finding nearest neighbors in vector space without keyword matching
actionable: Use vector databases (Pinecone, Weaviate, Milvus) for RAG implementations; add company documents as embeddings and query by semantic similarity

claim: Model Context Protocol (MCP) standardizes how LLMs connect to external tools and databases through a client-server architecture with authentication
actionable: Use MCP to connect LLMs to external systems; implement MCP servers for your company tools and databases to enable agent-based automation

claim: Context engineering combines few-shot prompting, RAG, and MCP to layer user preferences, retrieval, and tool access into LLM requests
actionable: Build context engineering pipelines that add user preferences (sliding window summaries), retrieved docs (RAG), and tool access (MCP) to every LLM request

claim: Agents are long-running processes that query LLMs and external tools repeatedly to complete multi-step user goals autonomously
actionable: Design agents as persistent services that can access LLMs, databases, and APIs multiple times to solve complex problems autonomously

claim: Reinforcement learning with human feedback (RLHF) trains models to prefer high-scoring responses by penalizing poor paths in the token vector space
actionable: Collect human feedback comparing model responses, assign scores, and use RLHF to reinforce preferred regions of the token generation space

claim: Chain of thought prompting trains models to reason step-by-step, enabling them to apply reasoning to harder problems with more intermediate steps
actionable: Train models with step-by-step breakdowns of reasoning; prompt production systems to show intermediate reasoning steps for better accuracy

claim: Reasoning models like DeepSeek and O1/O3 dynamically add reasoning steps based on problem complexity, using more steps for harder problems
actionable: Use reasoning models for complex multi-step problems where the model can decide how much reasoning is needed based on problem difficulty

claim: Multimodal models achieve better performance than text-only models because training on multiple modalities (images, audio, video) improves semantic understanding
actionable: Use multimodal models when available; include images, audio, or video inputs to improve understanding and performance over text-only approaches

claim: Small language models (3M-300M parameters) trained on proprietary or task-specific data enable on-device deployment with specialized performance
actionable: Build or deploy SLMs on company-specific data; use them for edge/on-device tasks where you need domain specialization without large model costs

claim: Knowledge distillation trains small models to mimic large models by having students match teacher outputs, condensing information into fewer parameters
actionable: Use large models to generate training data for small models; have small models match large model outputs on your domain to compress knowledge

claim: Quantization reduces model weight precision (32-bit to 8-bit) cutting memory usage by 75%, primarily reducing inference cost not training cost
actionable: Apply post-training quantization to deploy models efficiently; target 8-bit quantization for production inference to save memory and latency
